note:
司法部长兹比格涅夫·佐布罗（Zbigniew Ziobro）周四宣布了一项法律倡议，旨在使互联网用户能够就删除网上帖子以及设立言论自由特别法庭提出投诉。
  司法部长Zbigniew Ziobro表示，该法案的目的是使互联网用户感到自己的权利受到保护，并且他们的帖子不能从在线平台上任意删除。该法案的全名是关于表达自己的观点以及在互联网上搜索和传播信息的自由的法律。根据其规定，如果社交媒体服务上的内容未违反波兰法律，则不得删除其内容或屏蔽帐户。如果被移除或阻塞，可以将投诉发送到平台，该平台将有24小时的时间进行考虑。在做出决定后的48小时内，用户将能够向法院提出请愿书，要求其恢复使用权。法院将在收到投诉之日起7天内考虑投诉，整个过程将以电子方式进行。 ＆＃34;意识形态检查倾向的受害者通常还是在波兰活动的各个团体的代表，他们的内容被删除或屏蔽，只是因为他们表达观点并提及从社区角度来看是不可接受的价值观。 ..对社交媒体的功能产生越来越大的影响，＆＃34; Ziobro先生说。 ＆＃34;我们意识到这不是一个容易的话题，我们意识到在互联网上还应该为每个感到诽谤的人提供一个保证范围，对各种内容的限制范围可能会对它产生负面影响他人自由的领域，他说。 ＆＃34;但是我们想提出这样一种工具，该工具可以使一方和另一方都可以要求一个机构做出决定，该机构可以裁定此类社交媒体帐户中出现的内容是否确实侵犯了个人权利，是否可以消除它或是否存在审查制度。＆＃34;如果特别法庭作出有利于原告的裁决，并且互联网服务不服从该裁决，则可以对互联网服务处以电子通信办公室最高800万兹罗提（180万欧元）的罚款。
note_en:
Justice Minister Zbigniew Ziobro announced a legal initiative on Thursday aimed at enabling internet users to file complaints against the removal of online posts as well as the creation of a special court for freedom of speech.
  Justice Minister Zbigniew Ziobro said the aim of the bill was to give internet users the feeling that their rights are protected and that their posts cannot be arbitrarily removed from online platforms.   The full name of the bill is the law on freedom of expressing one's own views and searching and disseminating information on the internet.   Under its provisions, social media services will not be allowed to remove content or block accounts if the content on them does not break Polish law. In the event of removal or blockage, a complaint can be sent to the platform, which will have 24 hours to consider it. Within 48 hours of the decision, the user will be able to file a petition to the court for the return of access. The court will consider complaints within seven days of receipt and the entire process is to be electronic.   "Often, the victims of tendencies for ideological censorship are also representatives of various groups operating in Poland, whose content is removed or blocked, just because they express views and refer to values that are unacceptable from the point of view of communities... with an ever-stronger influence on the functioning of social media," Mr Ziobro said.    "We realise that it is not an easy topic, we realise that on the internet there should also be a sphere of guarantees for everybody who feels slandered, a sphere of limitation of various content which may carry with it a negative impact on the sphere of other people's freedom," he said. "But we would like to propose such tools that will enable both one side and the other to call for the decision of a body that will be able to adjudicate whether content appearing on such and such a social media account really violates personal rights, whether it can be eliminated, or whether there is censorship."   If a special court rules in favour of the plaintiff and the internet service does not obey the ruling it can subject the internet service to a fine of up to PLN 8 million (EUR 1.8 million) imposed by the Office of Electronic Communications.
title: 波兰司法部长宣布在线言论自由法案
title_en: Justice Minister in Poland announces online freedom of speech bill

您的互联网服务提供商是否每月用您自己的钱购买的电缆调制解调器或路由器向您收费？或者，也许您是否曾经因为无法逃避费用而费心购买这些物品？从12月20日（星期日）开始，该费用将是非法的，您应该告诉ISP您将不再容忍，威胁要起诉和/或利用其可能具有的任何具有约束力的仲裁条款。
 去年，国会通过了一项法律，该法律应该在2020年6月20日之前解决了这一荒谬的漏洞-尽管FCC设法通过废除一些有关电缆公司如何没有资源停止充电的废话来将截止日期延长了六个月您有钱了，法律明天就应该全面生效。
 请注意，法律的实际案文仍然允许某些BS发生。如果您的ISP向您发送了路由器，则需要将其退回以避免产生费用。
  Frontier尤其臭名昭著，因为它每月向客户收取10美元的设备使用费（无论您是否使用），这是该公司的话，不是我的，但Frontier清楚地知道它将不再能够这样做。从本月开始，该公司的设备页面已更改，删除了有关强制性费用的部分。如果您想比较的话，这是上个月的网站存档副本。
 如果您可以购买自己的电缆调制解调器，建议您这样做。调制解调器通常会在不到一年的时间内收回成本，而且ISP通常提供的多合一路由器盒通常提供的Wi-Fi覆盖范围比您自己可以轻松添加到房屋中的情况要差，尤其是现在使用网状Wi-Fi系统比以往任何时候都更好，更便宜，更容易使用。
  在美国，我们平均要支付亚洲和欧洲平均租用的调制解调器大约八到十七倍的费用，这也无济于事。这只是2021年美国互联网需要修复的方式之一。
note_en:
Is your internet service provider charging you every month for the cable modem or router that  you purchased with your own money? Or, perhaps, have you never bothered to buy those items because you couldn’t escape the fee? That fee will be illegal starting Sunday, December 20th, and you should tell your ISP that you’ll no longer tolerate it, threaten to sue, and/or take advantage of any binding arbitration clause it may have with you.
 Last year, Congress passed a law that should have fixed this ridiculous loophole as of June 20th, 2020 — and though the FCC  managed to extend the deadline six months by spinning up some bullshit about how cable companies  didn’t have the resources  to stop charging you money, the law should take full effect tomorrow.
 Do note that  the actual text of the law still allows some BS to occur. If your ISP sends you a router, you’ll need to return it to avoid charges.
  Frontier in particular has been notorious for charging customers $10 a month for their equipment “whether you use it or not” — the company’s words, not mine — but Frontier is clearly aware it won’t be able to do that anymore. Starting this month,  the company’s equipment page has changed to remove the part where it talks about the mandatory fee.  Here’s an archived copy of the site from last month if you want to compare.
 If you can purchase your own cable modem, I suggest you do. Modems will generally pay for themselves in less than a year, and the all-in-one router boxes that ISPs typically provide typically offer worse Wi-Fi coverage than you could easily add to a house yourself, particularly now that mesh Wi-Fi systems are better, cheaper, and easier to use than ever before.
  It also doesn’t help that in the US, we pay roughly eight to 17 times more to rent a modem on average that Asia and Europe do, respectively. That’s just  one of the ways America’s internet needs to be fixed in 2021.
title: 有线电视公司无法再“租用”您已经拥有的路由器
title_en: Cable companies can no longer ‘rent’ you the router you already own

note:
12月11日，该州的全屋服务订单打到太浩湖时，西尔维亚·瓦斯汀（Silvia Wasjutin）立即在圣诞节取消了在太浩市圣诞节预订Airbnb的预订。该地区基本上被关闭，并且不鼓励旅行。
  决定取消这次旅行令人心碎。这是Wasjutin，她的六个成年子女和同父异母的妹妹唯一一家团聚。但肯定的是，Wasjutin认为，鉴于停工令的突然发生及其对旅客和住宿提供者的广泛影响，她的房东将同意退还预订的全部6,600美元费用。
    房东是一名五十多岁的女性，她因对社区报复的担忧而要求匿名，她告诉《纪事报》，客人应该知道大流行期间的预订风险，而且Airbnb的取消政策允许她将部分费用保留在后期。取消。她告诉Wasjutin，在大流行期间，无论健康状况如何，游客都来了塔霍。但是Wasjutin不敢抗拒新秩序，甚至可能传播冠状病毒。
  通过Airbnb的信息服务进行交易后，Wasjutin取消了房东，并退还了$ 3,000。这两人在剩余的3600美元中处于僵持状态。
  随着新的停工令席卷整个加利福尼亚，湾区的许多人处于类似情况。塔霍（Tahoe）的酒店和物业经理将清理12月份的预订并退款。同时，Airbnb（本月在首次公开募股中筹集了数十亿美元）避免了调解分歧，即使这种分歧在大流行期间变得越来越普遍。
  Wasjutin希望向Airbnb的真实人解释她的情况会有所帮助。但是她努力地打电话给她。
     Wasjutin说：“当我最终与某人交谈时，他们对加利福尼亚发生的事情一无所知。” 
这场流行病对Airbnb的点对点市场模型进行了压力测试，在这个模型中，世界各地的宾客和房东只能独自应对前所未有的不确定性。该公司在上市时披露的财务数据表明，这种流行病是一个福音。从四月到六月，由于火山口，它损失了数十亿美元的收入，但随后的三个月预订量又恢复了。波士顿大学教授酒店营销和研究Airbnb租金的马卡兰德·莫迪（Makarand Mody）说，旅行者选择了长期的独立住宿。
  没有迹象表明该公司将在太浩湖等高需求地区解决挑战，在那里对短期租金的争执导致社区采取镇压措施。
  莫迪说：“如果他们在塔霍（Tahoe）做新的事情，他们可能必须在全国范围内这样做。” “因此，我认为他们不愿采取任何可能以任何方式损害其业务的措施。”
  与《纪事报》交谈的湾区客人说，塔霍（Tahoe）房东很同情，并在最后一刻取消预订时全额退款。
  对于主机，甚至发出取消通知也可能很复杂。塔霍（Tahoe）的一些人说，在停工期间删除其房源几乎是不可能的。
  塔霍市西湖房地产公司的总裁兼经纪人朱莉·韦德（Julie Weed）管理着Airbnb上约65家房地产。她说，当宣布关闭时，她无法以足够快的速度取消预订以将其商品清单从网站上撤下。即使是在家定购，对太浩的需求也太多了。
  “一旦取消预订请求，我会立即收到另一个请求，”韦德说。 “我给Airbnb打电话说，‘你能全盘关闭Tahoe吗？’他们说不，这取决于房东自行管理。” 
莫迪说，尽管许多旅行社在大流行期间提供了更为灵活的预订政策，但爱彼迎在酒店业仍然是一个例外。
  她说：“在COVID中，消费者的期望已经改变。” “我们希望酒店业的每个人都能更加灵活地满足我们的需求。航空公司和酒店都在这样做，您可以在最后一刻取消几乎任何费用。但是，Airbnb并没有真正效仿。”
     取而代之的是，该公司将客人引导至其“情节减轻”政策，该政策不涵盖与COVID订单或旅行咨询相关的旅行变更。实际上，这使房东和客人之间可以协商退款。
  Wasjutin的房东说，3月份，当Airbnb要求取消和退款时，她的收入受到了重创。她现在需要抵押贷款的钱和两个孩子的需要。
  主持人说：“这是我唯一的收入。” “我的丈夫在感恩节那天过世了，这是我养家糊口的唯一途径。”
    莫迪说：“只要他们向客户和主机提供信息，他们就会认为自己的法律责任就此终止。” “他们之所以能够摆脱困境，是因为没有适当的政府机构来追究他们的责任。”
  各个城市的短期租赁法规有时甚至相差很大。旧金山的规定很严格，而其他度假胜地的规定则较为宽松。 Airbnb与当地法规有联系，但其对法规的执行情况有所不同，司法管辖区已起诉迫使该公司采取行动。 
塔霍（Tahoe）的许多地区都在尝试规范解决方案。塔霍（Tahoe）长期以来一直是诸如滑雪小屋和湖边小屋之类的短期出租物业的温床，但是在过去的五年中，租金一直困扰着常年居住在该湖小社区中的当地人。许多人说，对于粗鲁的游客和有钱的第二房主来说，他们的家朝操场的方向倾斜得太远了。
  为了与之抗衡，南太浩湖选民两年前通过了一项措施，到2022年逐步淘汰大多数短期租金。包括塔霍（Tahoe）的西岸（West Shore）在内的普莱瑟县今年增加了短期租金登记制度，设定了安静的时间和占用率限制。特拉基的一项类似条例将于1月生效。
  这些条例不会让塔霍当局直接控制Airbnb。依从性仍将主要取决于主机，主机可能会面临罚款。
  Wasjutin的房东周五表示，如果重新预订该物业，她将考虑向Wasjutin退还全额退款。 Wasjutin说她对此表示怀疑，但已经决定对Airbnb做出决定。
  “说实话，”她说，“我不认为我会再做一次。”
   格雷戈里·托马斯（Gregory Thomas）是《旧金山纪事报》的生活方式和户外活动的编辑。电子邮件：gthomas@sfchronicle.com Twitter：@GregRThomas
note_en:
When the state’s  stay-at-home order hit Lake Tahoe on Dec. 11, Silvia Wasjutin immediately went to cancel her Airbnb booking for Christmas in Tahoe City at Christmas. The region was essentially shut down, and traveling was discouraged.
  Deciding to cancel the trip was heartbreaking. It’s the only time Wasjutin, her six adult children and her half-sister get together as a family. But surely, Wasjutin thought, given the suddenness of the shutdown order and its widespread effects on travelers and lodging providers, her host would agree to refund the full $6,600 cost of the reservation.
    The host, a woman in her 50s who asked to be anonymous over concern about reprisals from her community, told The Chronicle that guests should know the risks of booking during the pandemic and that Airbnb’s cancellation policy allows her to keep some of the cost on late cancellations. She told Wasjutin that tourists had come to Tahoe throughout the pandemic, regardless of health orders. But Wasjutin wasn’t comfortable defying the new order and perhaps spreading the coronavirus.
  After exchanges through Airbnb’s messaging service, Wasjutin canceled and the host refunded $3,000. The two have a stalemate over the remaining $3,600.
  With new shutdown orders sweeping across California, many people in the Bay Area are in similar situations. Hotels and property managers in Tahoe are  clearing bookings for December and giving refunds. Meanwhile, Airbnb — which  raised billions in an initial public offering this month — has avoided mediating disagreements even as they become more common during the pandemic.
  Wasjutin hoped explaining her circumstances to an actual human at Airbnb would help. But she struggled to get one on the phone.
     “When I finally talked to a person, they had no clue about what’s happening in California,” Wasjutin said.
  The pandemic presents a stress test of Airbnb’s peer-to-peer marketplace model, in which guests and hosts around the world are left to navigate unprecedented uncertainty on their own. Financial data the company disclosed while going public suggests the pandemic has been a boon. It lost billions of dollars in revenue from April to June as travel cratered, but bookings bounced back the following three months. Travelers have opted for long-term, self-contained lodging, said Makarand Mody, who teaches hospitality marketing at Boston University and studies Airbnb rentals.
  There are no signs the company will address challenges at high-demand locations like Lake Tahoe, where contention over short-term rentals has led communities to clamp down.
  “If they do something new in Tahoe, they might have to do that across the country,” Mody said. “So I think they’re hesitant about making any moves that might hurt their business in any way.”
  Bay Area guests who spoke with The Chronicle said Tahoe hosts were sympathetic and offered full refunds on last-minute cancellations.
  For hosts, even issuing cancellations can be complicated. Some in Tahoe say removing their listings during shutdown periods has been nearly impossible.
  Julie Weed, president and broker at Westlake Properties in Tahoe City, manages about 65 properties on Airbnb. When the shutdown was announced, she couldn’t cancel bookings fast enough to pull her listings off the website, she said. There’s too much demand to travel to Tahoe, even during the stay-at-home order.
  “As soon as I’d cancel a booking request, I’d immediately get another,” Weed said. “I called Airbnb and said, ‘Can you just shut down Tahoe across the board?’ They said no, it’s up to the hosts to manage on their own.”
      While many travel operators offer more flexible booking policies during the pandemic, Airbnb remains an outlier in the hospitality industry, Mody said.
  “In COVID, consumers’ expectations have changed,” she said. “We expect everyone in the hospitality industry to be more flexible to our needs. Airlines and hotels are doing it, where you can cancel pretty much last-minute without any charges. But Airbnb hasn’t really followed suit.”
     Instead, the company directs guests to its extenuating circumstances policy, which says it does not cover trip changes related to COVID orders or travel advisories. In effect, that leaves hosts and guests to negotiate refunds among themselves.
  Wasjutin’s host said her income took a big hit in March when Airbnb mandated cancellations and refunds. She now needs the money for her mortgage and the needs of two children.
  “This is my only income,” the host said. “My husband died a couple Thanksgivings ago and this is the only way I can feed my family.”
    “As long as they provide the information to customers and hosts, they see their legal responsibility as ending there,” Mody said. “They’ve been able to get away with it because there’s not a government structure in place to hold them accountable.”
  Short-term rental regulations vary, sometimes dramatically, from city to city. San Francisco’s rules are strict, while other vacation destinations have looser rules. Airbnb links to local regulations, but its enforcement of the rules varies, and jurisdictions have sued to force the company to act.
  Many Tahoe areas are  trying to regulate solutions. Tahoe has long been a hotbed of short-term rental properties like ski cabins and lakeside lodges, but in the past five years, rentals have become a headache for locals who reside in the lake’s small communities year-round. Many say their home has swung too far in the direction of a playground for rude tourists and wealthy second-homeowners.
  To combat it, South Lake Tahoe voters passed a measure two years ago to phase out most short-term rentals by 2022. Placer County, including Tahoe’s West Shore, added a registration system this year for short-term rentals, setting quiet hours and occupancy limits. A similar ordinance in Truckee takes effect in January.
  Those ordinances won’t give Tahoe authorities direct control over Airbnbs. Compliance still will be largely left up to hosts, who could face fines.
  Wasjutin’s host said Friday that she will consider issuing Wasjutin a full refund on her cancellation if the property is rebooked. Wasjutin said she’s skeptical, but has made up her mind about Airbnb.
  “Honestly,” she said, “I don’t think I’d do it again.”
   Gregory Thomas is The San Francisco Chronicle’s editor of lifestyle and outdoors. Email:  gthomas@sfchronicle.com Twitter:  @GregRThomas

我12岁的女儿梅根（Meghan）宣布她“绝对是男孩”。戴维森女士说，她的孩子从来都不是假小子，但在写给不列颠哥伦比亚省儿童医院的转诊信之前，家庭医生向她表示祝贺并询问她选择了什么代词。戴维森女士说：“我们以为我们要去找心理学家，但是那是一名护士和一名社工。”（她和女儿的名字都被更改了）。 “他们在十分钟之内给了我们的孩子卢普龙”（一种阻止青春期的药物）。 “他们直接与我们面前的孩子一起带走了毒品，而没有先与我们私下讨论。”没有提及其他心理健康问题，已知这些问题会增加性别不安的可能性，即您身在错误身体中的感觉。 “没有提供任何疗法，当我们提出它时，我们只是被甩在一边。”
 梅根（Meghan）属于近年来在西方世界中被确认为变性者的一波儿童。美国在2007年开设了一家性别诊所；如今，它已超过50个。世界各地的零星证据表明，在这类诊所中表现出性别焦虑症的儿童中，四分之三是青春期女孩，而直到最近，这一比例还是大致相等。越来越多的人正在过渡，选择恢复其以前的性别。不幸的是，如果孩子们已经开始了包括激素治疗在内的医学过渡，那么这可能会使他们不育，无法拥有完整的性生活。
  本月初，伦敦高等法院审理了一名名叫Keira Bell的流离失所者的案子，他对Tavistock诊所进行了司法审查，Tavistock诊所是英格兰唯一的青年性别认同中心。她声称诊所不应该允许她服用青春期阻滞剂，之后再接受睾丸激素治疗和双乳切除术。法院裁定，“极不可能”的是一个13岁且“怀疑” 14岁和15岁的孩子已经成熟到可以接受这种程序的程度，并且医生正在治疗16岁和17岁的孩子-开始前，老年人可能还需要咨询法官。
 反激进主义者认为，一个边缘化的群体正在流行文化中发声。他们的批评者反驳说，易受伤害的青少年正在一个在线世界迷失自我，这个世界把任何一个跨性别的人都吸引了。两者都可能是正确的。梅根（Meghan）的年轻同胞说：“挺直很无聊。”
 社会正在努力取得平衡。一些感觉自己身材不对的孩子会总是有这种感觉，并且可能会从改变自己的身体中受益。其他人会改变主意，其中许多人简直就是同性恋。医学检验无法区分这两个群体。患有心理健康问题或自闭症等疾病的儿童更容易出现性别焦虑症。弄清所有这些都是非常困难的。
 但是，有人担心富裕国家的平衡不正确。荷尔蒙和外科手术处方所依据的荷兰学者之一说，她的研究正在应用于未为其设计的年轻人。越来越多的人对此表示反对。 《经济学人》采访了讲英语的富裕国家的四十多人，其中包括跨性别者，父母，医生，社会工作者，老师以及在儿童时期被确定为跨性别者。大多数批评者都希望匿名，因为他们担心失去工作或在Twitter上被烙上烙印。
  一位加拿大儿科医生说：“医学的首要职责是'不要伤害'。” “在任何其他医学领域，如果您因改变身体的手术和两性激素而导致永久性不育，则最好拥有一些相当强大的数据……但是我们已经走了这条路，当时没有强大的数据所有。” 
寻找最佳方法将需要辩论。但是，一些激进主义者不欢迎辩论。戴维森女士说：“我们是自由主义者。” “但我们总是被人觉得自己是提出问题的右翼骗子。”
  尚无关于儿童跨性别病例发生率的全球统计数据。十年来，伦敦Tavistock的推荐人数激增了30倍，去年有2700名儿童被推荐。被推荐的人中有将近一半将开始使用青春期阻滞剂。在2019-20年，其性别诊所治疗了382名患者，高于2016-17年的123名。美国不发布统计数据。然而，疾病控制中心（Centers for Disease Control）在2017年对美国高中生的一项调查中，有1.8％的人表示自己是变性人，另有1.6％的人表示自己不确定。
 青春期阻滞剂的情况是，它们可以帮助患有严重性别焦虑症的儿童，他们对建立“错误的”性别特征感到绝望。那是因为这些药物可以使他们免于痛苦，并且可能在以后避免创伤性干预：双乳腺切除术；子宫切除术或亚当苹果的剃毛。
 许多经历了完全医疗过渡的人说，他们对结果感到满意。住在温哥华的Tru Wilson是其中之一。 Tru是个温柔的男孩，Tru的父母认为他们的孩子可能是同性恋。然后，他们一起看了一个关于跨性别孩子的节目，Tru说：“就是我！”现年17岁的Tru于12岁开始接受阻断剂治疗，于14岁开始接受雌激素治疗，并有望在明年内接受手术。她说：“我对自己的旅程感到很遗憾，”。她的父亲加菲猫（Garfield）给医院的医生留下了深刻的印象。 “没有压力迫使我们去做我们认为不适合我们女儿的事情。”其他许多父母也报告了积极的经历。他说，他们认真对待青春期阻滞剂，所有患者“都经过了严格的评估，包括确认他们有能力考虑其利弊”。
 但是其他过渡者开始将此类程序视为错误。克莱尔（她的真名）现在是佛罗里达州的一名19岁学生，由于不喜欢她的身体，因此开始使用14岁的睾丸激素。 （她也感到非常沮丧。）“我觉得这是唯一的选择，特别是坚持认为烦躁不安意味着您无法挽回地跨性别，因此，如果您不过渡，很可能会自杀。”她说，获取激素很容易。 “他们几乎给我盖了金印。”然后，在17岁时，她的烦躁情绪消失了。 “我感到非常失落。我从未听说过这种情况。”她摆脱了睾丸激素，拥抱了自己作为女同性恋的身份，并且非常生气。 “是医疗行业和对烦躁不安的人们的普遍社会态度使我失望。”
 这种“抵制”似乎很普遍。至少有六项医学研究表明，在成年前，与性别有关的困扰的儿童中，有61％至98％能够适应其出生性别。但是，所有这些研究都是针对患有早发型烦躁不安的儿童的。一项有关女孩青春期烦躁的最新研究表明，在许多情况下，这种烦恼是由互联网的影响，已转型的女性朋友以及青春期的痛苦所致。 “需要进行的是对女孩青春期烦躁不安的质量研究，以及与自闭症和心理健康诊断的重叠，”国际医生国际循证性别医学学会内分泌学家兼主任威尔·马洛说。和研究人员。
 停止治疗的决定对已经接受治疗的人来说是最困难的。费城的荣格治疗师丽莎·玛奇亚诺（Lisa Marchiano）为数名此类人士提供咨询。他们所有人都认为他们过早获得医疗干预。她说：“承认您为错误的策略投入了巨大的力量，这需要巨大的力量。” 
支持医疗的证据也在受到挑战。向烦躁不安的青少年提供激素和手术的争论很大程度上取决于荷兰开创的一种干预方法，该方法已被称为“荷兰协议”。对55名患有早发性烦躁不安的年轻人进行了测试。这些少年接受了青春期阻滞剂，异性激素治疗，并在18岁以后接受了手术。没有对照组。取而代之的是，2014年发表的对该方法的研究结果得出的结论是，基于至少在手术后一年的心理功能，这些医疗干预是成功的。
 作者警告说，他们的论文只包含一个小样本，仅测量短期的心理结果，而没有评估其对身体健康的影响。它的一位研究人员Annelou de Vries今年在医学杂志《儿科学》上发表了一篇评论，说这种方法被错误地应用于患有青春期烦躁不安的儿童（主要是女孩）。她强调有必要确定那些需要加强精神保健支持而不是重新分配性别的人。牛津大学循证医学中心的教授卡尔·海内汉（Carl Heneghan）去年写道，荷兰协议的使用相当于“对儿童的不受管制的实时实验”。英格兰高等法院也称此类干预为“实验性”。青春期荷尔蒙泛滥，以医生无法完全理解的方式帮助孩子与性别进行和解。阻止者阻止了这一点。
   Tavistock诊所认为青春期阻滞剂是可逆的。在一定程度上是正确的。但是，它们会影响骨骼密度，因此医生通常希望将患者转移到具有更持久影响的异性激素上。法院得出结论，阻滞剂几乎总是导致荷尔蒙，荷尔蒙具有健康风险。睾丸激素增加了心脏疾病的机会。它导致阴道和子宫萎缩，这可能在以后的生活中需要进行子宫切除术。
 尽管存在不确定性，但许多医生还是接受了医疗干预。标准方法以前是“观察等待”，它提倡在进行激素和手术之前进行咨询。但是，纽约西奈山变性医学和外科中心的约书亚·塞弗尔（Joshua Safer）表示，青春期阻滞剂现在是“保守的选择”，因为它们让孩子们有时间决定自己想做什么。包括世界跨性别健康专业协会（）在内的医学机构现在表示，确认一个人的跨性别身份是“国际最佳实践”。
 在美国，2010年的《平价医疗法案》（Affordable Care Act）加强了干预，该法案禁止健康保险公司基于性取向和性别认同进行歧视。实际上，他们必须为声称自己是反式的人提供激素，就像她们为妇女提供避孕激素一样。
 2018年，美国儿科学会（）表示，所有医学证据均支持“肯定性”治疗方法。但是，根据加拿大性行为科学家詹姆斯·坎特尔（James Cantor）的详细反驳，该主题的11项学术研究均未得出该结论。
 很多医生甚至都没有遵守指南。俄勒冈太平洋大学心理学教授劳拉·爱德华兹·利珀（Laura Edwards-Leeper）曾帮助在波士顿建立了美国第一家面向儿童和青少年的跨性别诊所。是反式的”。她说，理想情况下，一个患有性别不安的青少年应该定期去看治疗师，他鼓励他们探索其他可能导致他们的感觉的原因，并在接受阻断剂或激素治疗之前进行全面的心理评估。她说：“即使发生其中一件事情，也很少见。” 
诊所中的肯定常会在学校中回响肯定。加拿大和一些澳大利亚州禁止基于自我宣布的性别身份歧视任何人。主要的学校课程称为-123，该课程在不列颠哥伦比亚省和艾伯塔省教授，并将在加拿大各地推广。该计划的许多内容都没有争议，关于友善和反对欺凌。但是批评家担心，这会使质疑孩子的决定变得困难。
 运营名为“加拿大性别报告”的网站的帕梅拉·布冯（Pamela Buffone）表示，此类计划将“性别认同”的概念（即生物学上的男性可以识别为女性，或者女性识别为男性的概念）附加到了更熟悉的“性取向”（同性恋或异性恋）。去年3月，布冯女士针对渥太华一家学校董事会的一项课程提出了一项法律申诉，该课程是在另一项计划下进行的。在该课程中，她说她的六岁女儿没有男孩和女孩这样的事情。
  支持新课程的人说，在学校教跨性别问题很重要，就像在种族或宗教方面讲授一样重要。加拿大老师格伦·汉斯曼（Glen Hansman）曾在实施中发挥作用，他说在学校里肯定代词和名字并不是“通往其他事物的门户药”。 Vince是加拿大农村的一名18岁跨性别男孩，但他的真实姓名也不是，这是许多年轻跨性别者的生命线。他希望该计划在他的学校中存在，他说他因性别不符合而受到殴打。
 许多立法者也不想支持自己，但也都表示支持。看到该州如何使同性恋者失败后，他们确定该州不应重复跨性别者的错误。乔·拜登（Joe Biden）在美国已承诺将《平等法》签署为法律。这将大大打击在住房和工作场所等对跨性别者的普遍歧视。但这也重新定义了性别以包括性别认同。可以理解为这样一种观点，即应该以所选择的身份确认儿童并接受治疗，即使该身份可能只是暂时的。
 在澳大利亚，首都堪培拉和昆士兰州已将与性取向或性别认同有关的“转换疗法”定为违法。美国的某些州也是如此。加拿大正在考虑类似的法律。这将两个独立的问题融合在一起。许多人会说，试图将同性恋者转变为异性恋是错误的。但是，反式转换疗法的隐含定义可能使任何可以帮助儿童确定烦躁不安是永久性还是阶段性的烦恼以及对症下药的方法都违法了。
 反弹开始了。在瑞典，在2008-18年度13至17岁女孩的性别焦虑症诊断上升了1,500％之后，更多的媒体报道集中在儿童过渡问题上。活动家Aleksa Lundberg说，如果她今天有同样的选择，她可能不会接受手术。儿童转诊到性别诊所的人数一年下降了65％。芬兰最近发布了更严格的指南，建议对早发和青春期烦躁不安采取不同的治疗方法，并鼓励患者寻求咨询。
 在美国，反激进主义者将有关待遇的问题视为政治问题。美国公民自由联盟的跨性别律师Chase Strangio在推特上对英国法院的判决发表了推文：“请看清它的含义-试图为我们的幸福，我们的希望和我们对我们的身体的爱武器化。这是对跨性别生存的危险攻击，并且正在蔓延。” 
美国保守的一些州的政客制定了法案，使医生给孩子开青春期阻滞剂或激素是违法的。这在很大程度上是煽动文化战争的一种尝试，但它也反映了一些父母的担忧。
 布冯女士说，她对女儿的学校和地方当局表示了担忧。 “好像我已经离开加拿大，来到某种威权国家。他们说这就是我们正在做的事情，很明显我没有追索权。”魁北克一些有自己课程的父母也反对。顾问凯瑟琳（Catherine）要求看她六岁的性教育课的内容时，学校拒绝了，所以她提出了信息自由的要求。事实证明，老师被告知“孩子们可以开始探索3至7岁之间的性别认同”，并且性别在出生时就被“分配”了，而不是被观察到。
  除非父母不同意，否则澳大利亚家庭法院近年来已不再作出有关给予阻断剂和激素甚至为青少年手术的决定。取而代之的是，它最近看到了第一例儿童被从不支持过渡的父母那里带走。该裁决几乎没有在新闻界报道。
  昆士兰大学法学院院长帕特里克·帕金森（Patrick Parkinson）说，贝尔女士在英格兰的判决意味着，这类父母将有反对女儿被遣送的依据。他认为，医生声称青春期阻滞剂是可逆的，对人体没有危害。他说：“这对澳大利亚的医学界来说是一个巨大的警钟。”
 但是，对于美国跨性别诊所的许多医生而言，限制儿童使用青春期阻滞剂的想法令人厌恶。洛杉矶儿童医院青少年健康与发展中心的约翰娜·奥尔森·肯尼迪（Johanna Olson-Kennedy）说，她为失去英语儿童的“这一令人难以置信的工具”感到遗憾。悉尼大学最近退休的心理学教授戴安娜·肯尼（Dianna Kenny）说：“我认为将会有大量的诉讼。” “但是他们将无法及时挽救被错误诊断为跨性别的一代青少年。”
 至于戴维森女士，女儿梅根仍然在抑郁症中挣扎。但是她决定和父母一起不接受卢普龙。 5月，那时14岁，她宣布：“妈妈，我决定我是女孩。”她化妆很多，然后去购物中心给指甲涂了油漆。但是经历使她的母亲变成了激进主义者。她已经与一个妇女团体签约，该团体倡导基于生物性别的权利。她说：“我生气得要命。” ■
 本文出现在印刷版的“国际”部分，标题为“男孩和女孩”。

I  12-year-old daughter, Meghan, announced she was “definitely a boy”. Ms Davidson says her child was never a tomboy but the family doctor congratulated her and asked what pronouns she had chosen, before writing a referral to the British Columbia Children’s Hospital ( ). “We thought we were going to see a psychologist, but it was a nurse and a social worker,” says Ms Davidson (both her and her daughter’s names have been changed). “Within ten minutes they had offered our child Lupron”—a puberty-blocking drug. “They brought up the drug directly with our child, in front of us, without discussing it with us privately first.” There was no mention of other mental-health issues, which are known to increase the likelihood of gender dysphoria, the feeling that you are in the wrong body. “There was no therapy on offer and we were just brushed aside when we raised it.”
 Meghan belongs to a wave of children across the Western world who have identified as transgender in recent years. America had one gender clinic in 2007; now it has more than 50. Piecemeal evidence around the world suggests that three-quarters of children expressing gender dysphoria at such clinics are adolescent girls, whereas until recently it was roughly evenly split. An increasing number are also de-transitioning, choosing to revert to their previous gender. Unfortunately, if children have already begun a medical transition, including hormone treatment, it can leave them infertile and unable to have a full sex life.
  Earlier this month the High Court in London looked at the case of one detransitioner, Keira Bell, who had brought a judicial review against the Tavistock clinic, England’s only specialist youth gender-identity centre. She claimed that the clinic should not have allowed her to take puberty blockers and later undergo testosterone treatment and a double mastectomy. The court ruled that it was “highly unlikely” that a 13-year-old and “doubtful” that 14- and 15-year-olds are mature enough to consent to such a procedure, and that doctors treating 16- and 17-year-olds may also need to consult a judge before starting.
 Trans activists argue that a long-marginalised group is now finding its voice in popular culture. Their critics retort that vulnerable teenagers are losing themselves in an online world which adulates anyone who comes out as trans. Both could be right. “Being straight is boring,” says Meghan’s younger sibling.
 Society is struggling to strike a balance. Some children who feel they are in the wrong body will always feel that way and might benefit from altering their bodies. Others will change their minds—many of these will simply turn out to be gay. No medical test can tell these two groups apart. Children with mental-health problems or conditions such as autism are more likely to experience gender dysphoria. Untangling all this is extremely hard.
 However, there are worries that rich countries have the balance wrong. One of the Dutch scholars on whose work the prescribing of hormones and surgery is based has said that her research is being applied to young people for whom it was not designed. And a growing number of people are dissenting.  The Economist spoke to more than four dozen people in rich English-speaking countries, including trans people, parents, doctors, social workers, teachers and people who had identified as trans when they were children. Most of those who were critical wanted to be anonymous for fear of losing their jobs or being branded bigots on Twitter.
  “The first duty of medicine is ‘Do no harm’,” says a Canadian paediatrician. “In any other branch of medicine, if you were causing permanent sterility with body-altering surgery and cross-sex hormones, you had better have some pretty strong data...But we’re already going down that road with no strong data at all.”
 To find the best approach will require debate. Some activists do not welcome debate, however. “We are liberal people,” says Ms Davidson. “But we are always made to feel like we are right-wing crackpots for raising questions.”
  Nobody has global statistics for the rate of trans cases among children. Referrals to the Tavistock in London have surged 30-fold in a decade, with 2,700 children referred there last year. Nearly half those referred will start on puberty blockers. In 2019-20, the   treated 382 patients in its gender clinic, up from 123 in 2016-17. America does not publish statistics. However, in a survey of American high-school students in 2017 by the Centres for Disease Control 1.8% said they were transgender and a further 1.6% said they were unsure.
 The case for puberty blockers is that they can help children with severe gender dysphoria, who feel desperate about developing the “wrong” sex characteristics. That is because the drugs could spare them distress and, potentially, traumatic interventions later: a double mastectomy; a hysterectomy or the shaving of the Adam’s apple.
 Many who go through full medical transition say they are happy with the result. Tru Wilson, who lives in Vancouver, is one. Tru was a gentle boy, and Tru’s parents thought their child might be gay. They then watched a programme together on trans kids and Tru said, “That’s me!” Tru, now 17, began on blockers at 12, on oestrogen at 14, and is expecting to go through surgery within the next year. “I have zero regrets on how my journey went,” she says. Her father, Garfield, has been impressed by physicians at the  . “There was no pressure pushing us to do anything that we didn’t feel was right for our daughter.” Many other parents also report positive experiences.   says that they take the use of puberty blockers seriously and all their patients “go through rigorous assessments including confirmation that they are capable of considering the benefits and risks”.
 But other transitioners come to see such procedures as a mistake. Claire (not her real name), now a 19-year-old student in Florida, started on testosterone aged 14 because of a loathing for her body. (She was also deeply depressed.) “I felt it was the only option, especially with the insistence that having dysphoria meant you are irrevocably trans and thus you will probably kill yourself if you don’t transition.” Obtaining hormones was easy, she says. “They pretty much gold-stamped me through.” Then, aged 17, her dysphoria disappeared. “I felt extremely lost. I had never heard of this happening.” She came off testosterone, embraced her identity as a lesbian, and is furious. “It is the medical industry and the general social attitude towards dysphoric people that failed me.”
 Such “desistance” appears to be common. At least half a dozen medical studies show that between 61% and 98% of children presenting with gender-related distress were reconciled to their natal sex before adulthood. However, all these studies looked at children with early-onset dysphoria. One recent study on adolescent dysphoria among girls suggested that in many cases it is brought on by the influence of the internet, by female friends who have transitioned and by the miseries of puberty. “What is needed is quality research into adolescent-onset dysphoria among girls, and the overlap with autism and mental-health diagnoses,” says Will Malone, an endocrinologist and director at the Society for Evidence-Based Gender Medicine, an international group of doctors and researchers.
 The decision to desist is hardest for those who have received medical treatment. Lisa Marchiano, a Jungian therapist in Philadelphia, counsels several such people. They all believe they were given access to medical interventions too soon. “It takes enormous strength to admit you have invested so much in a strategy that is a mistake,” she says.
 The evidence in favour of medical treatment is being challenged, too. Arguments for providing hormones and surgery to dysphoric teenagers lean heavily on an intervention approach pioneered in the Netherlands, which has come to be known as “the Dutch protocol”. This was tested on 55 young people with early-onset dysphoria. The teenagers were treated with puberty blockers, cross-sex hormones and, after they turned 18, surgery. There was no control group. Instead the results of a study of the approach, published in 2014, concluded that these medical interventions were successful on the basis of psychological functioning at least one year after surgery.
 The authors warn that their paper contains a small sample, measures only short-term psychological outcomes and has no evaluation of the implications for physical health. One of its researchers, Annelou de Vries, this year published a commentary in  Pediatrics, a medical journal, saying that the approach is being wrongly applied to children (mostly girls) with adolescent-onset dysphoria. She emphasised the need to identify those who need enhanced mental-health support, rather than gender reassignment. Carl Heneghan, a professor at the Centre for Evidence-Based Medicine at Oxford University, wrote last year that use of the Dutch protocol amounts to an “unregulated live experiment on children”. The High Court in England also called such interventions “experimental”. The flood of hormones in puberty help reconcile a child to their sex in a way that doctors do not fully understand. Blockers stop that.
   The Tavistock clinic argued that puberty blockers are reversible. That is true up to a point. However, they can affect bone density and so doctors often want to move patients on to cross-sex hormones, which have more permanent effects. The court concluded that blockers almost always lead on to hormones, which carry health risks. Testosterone heightens the chance of heart problems. It leads to vaginal and uterine atrophy which can make a hysterectomy necessary in later life.
 Despite the uncertainties, many doctors have embraced medical intervention. The standard approach used to be “watchful waiting”, which advocates counselling before moving on to hormones and surgery. However, Joshua Safer of the Mount Sinai Centre for Transgender Medicine and Surgery in New York says puberty blockers are now “the conservative option” because they allow children time to decide what they want to do. Medical bodies including the World Professional Association for Transgender Health ( ) now say that affirming a person’s transgender identity is “international best practice”.
 In America intervention was boosted by the Affordable Care Act of 2010, which banned health insurers from discriminating on the basis of sexual orientation and gender identity. In effect, they were thus obliged to cover hormones for people who say they are trans just as they provide contraceptive hormones for women.
 In 2018 the American Association of Pediatrics ( ) said that all medical evidence supports the “affirmative” approach. But according to a detailed rebuttal by James Cantor, a Canadian sexual-behaviour scientist, none of the 11 academic studies of the subject reaches that conclusion.
 Plenty of doctors fail to observe even  ’s guidelines. Laura Edwards-Leeper, a professor of psychology at Pacific University in Oregon who helped found America’s first transgender clinic for children and teens in Boston, says she gets many emails from parents “desperate to find a therapist who will not just blindly affirm that their child is trans”. Ideally, she said, an adolescent with gender dysphoria would have been regularly seeing a therapist, who encouraged them to explore other possible causes for their feelings and had a comprehensive psychological assessment before being put on blockers or hormones. “It is very rare that even one of these things happens,” she says.
  Affirmation in the clinic often echoes affirmation at school. Canada and some Australian states forbid discrimination against anyone on the basis of their self-declared gender identity. The main school programme, taught in British Columbia and Alberta and due to be rolled out across Canada, is called  -123. Much of the   programme is uncontroversial, about being kind and opposing bullying. But critics worry it makes questioning a child’s decisions difficult.
 Pamela Buffone, who runs a website called Canadian Gender Report, says that such programmes attach the concept of “gender identity” (the idea that a biological male can identify as a woman, or a female as a man) to the more familiar concept of “sexual orientation” (being gay or straight). In March last year Ms Buffone launched a legal complaint against a school board in Ottawa over a lesson, under a different programme, in which she says her six-year-old daughter was taught that there is no such thing as boys and girls.
  People who support the new curriculum say that it is important to teach trans issues in school just as it is important to teach about race or religion. Glen Hansman, a Canadian teacher who was instrumental in the implementation of   says that affirming pronouns and names in schools is “not a gateway drug to other things”. Vince, an 18-year-old trans boy in rural Canada, (also not his real name) says that   is a lifeline for many young trans people. He wishes the programme had existed in his school, where he says he was assaulted for being gender non-conforming.
 Many legislators, not wanting to look bigoted, are supportive, too. Having seen how the state failed gay people, they are determined that it should not repeat the mistake with trans people. In America Joe Biden has promised to sign the Equality Act into law. That will do a lot to combat widespread discrimination against trans people, such as in housing and the workplace. But it also redefines sex to include gender identity. That could be read to endorse the idea that children should be affirmed in the identity they choose and receive treatment for it—even if that identity may turn out to be temporary.
 In Australia the capital, Canberra, and the state of Queensland have outlawed “conversion therapy” in relation to sexual orientation or gender identity. So too have some American states. Canada is considering a similar law. This conflates two separate issues. Many people would say it is wrong to try to convert gay people into being straight. But the implicit definition of trans conversion therapy risks outlawing any counselling that helps children decide whether their dysphoria is permanent or a phase, and what to do about it.
 A backlash is beginning. In Sweden, after a 1,500% rise in gender dysphoria diagnoses among 13- to 17-year-old girls in 2008-18, more media coverage has focused on the problems of children transitioning. Aleksa Lundberg, an activist, said that she would probably not undergo surgery if she had the same choice today. Referrals of children to gender clinics have fallen by 65% in a year. Finland recently released stricter guidelines, recommending different treatment for early-onset and adolescent-onset dysphoria, and encouraging patients to seek counselling.
 In America trans activists see questions about treatment as political. Chase Strangio, a trans lawyer at the American Civil Liberties Union, tweeted of the English court’s decision: “Please see this for what it is—an attempt to weaponise our happiness, our hopefulness, and our love of our bodies. This is a dangerous attack on trans survival and it is spreading.”
 Some politicians in conservative American states have drawn up bills that would make it illegal for doctors to prescribe puberty blockers or hormones to children. This is largely an attempt to inflame the culture wars, but it also reflects the worries of some parents.
 Ms Buffone says she raised concerns with her daughter’s school and the local authority. “It was as though I had left Canada and arrived in some kind of authoritarian state. They said this is what we are doing and it was clear I had no recourse.” Some parents in Quebec, which has its own curriculum, are also objecting. When Catherine, a consultant, asked to see the content of her six-year-old’s sex-education class, the school refused, so she made a freedom-of-information request. It turned out teachers are told that “Children can begin to explore their gender identity between the ages of 3 and 7” and that sex is “assigned” at birth rather than observed.
  The Australian Family Court has in recent years removed itself from decisions about giving blockers and hormones and even surgery for teenagers, unless parents disagree. Instead, it has recently seen the first case of a child being removed from parents who did not support transition. The ruling was hardly reported in the press.
  Patrick Parkinson, dean of law at the University of Queensland, says Ms Bell’s judgment in England means that such parents will have a basis to oppose their daughter’s removal. He thinks doctors’ claims that puberty blockers are reversible and do no harm have been debunked. “This is a massive wake-up call for the medical profession in Australia,” he says.
 However for many doctors in transgender clinics in America, the idea of restricting the use of puberty blockers in children is anathema. Johanna Olson-Kennedy of the Centre for Transyouth Health and Development at Children’s Hospital Los Angeles says she mourns the loss of “this incredible tool” for English children. “I think there is going to be an avalanche of lawsuits,” says Dianna Kenny, recently retired professor of psychology at the University of Sydney. “But they won’t be in time to save a generation of adolescents who have been wrongly diagnosed as being trans.”
 As for Ms Davidson, daughter Meghan still struggles with depression. However she decided, with her parents, not to take the Lupron. In May, by then 14, she announced: “Mum, I’ve decided I’m a girl.” She put on lots of make-up and went to the shopping mall to get her nails painted. But the experience has turned her mother into an activist. She has signed up with   a women’s group that advocates for rights to be based on biological sex. “I’m mad as hell,” she says. ■
 This article appeared in the International section of the print edition under the headline "Boys and girls"
title: 英国对跨性别青少年的裁决可能会产生全球影响
title_en: An English ruling on transgender teens could have global repercussions

 众议院监督与改革委员会成员，国会女议员黛比·瓦瑟曼·舒尔茨（Debbie Wasserman Schultz）在9月3日试图进行检查时，被拒绝进入佛罗里达州的两个邮局。[91]
  2020年12月，假期的包裹热潮导致全国范围内普遍的包裹运输延迟。美国邮政总局的设施变得不知所措：印第安纳波利斯配送中心的一位主管报告说，为了方便起见，工人只是将[包裹]扔进卡车而不进行扫描＆＃34;进入系统； [92]在宾夕法尼亚州的阿伦敦，邮件没有空位而坐在停车场中，费城的业务员告诉费城询问者＆＃34;现在不要使用邮局，因为我们可以不会发送邮件＆＃34;。 [93] 12月下旬，USPS以COVID-19问题和劳动力短缺为由，承认其网站上的延误，鼓励提早寄出包裹，但是对于许多已经被包裹在全国配送中心的包裹来说，这太少了，太迟了。 [94] [95] [96] [97]

Jump to navigation  Jump to search  The  2020 United States Postal Service crisis is a series of events that have caused backlogs and delays in the delivery of  mail by the  United States Postal Service (USPS). The crisis stems primarily from changes implemented by  Postmaster General  Louis DeJoy shortly after taking office in June 2020.  [1]  [2]  [3] The delays have had substantial legal, political, economic, and health repercussions.  [1]  [2]  [4]
 There is controversy and speculation about whether the delays are unintended consequences of restructuring operations, or if they were intentionally created for political and/or financial gain. DeJoy has supported and donated to  President  Donald Trump, who has publicly linked his opposition to emergency funding for the Postal Service to his desire to restrict  voting by mail in the  2020 elections.  [5]  [6]
 On August 18, 2020, under heavy political and legal pressure, DeJoy announced that he would be "suspending" the policy changes until after the  November 2020 election.  [7] He testified to the  Senate on August 21, and to the  House of Representatives on August 24, concerning the changes and their effects.  [1]
   The USPS has had a history of  budget deficits dating back to a 2006 law requiring it to prepay retiree  health benefits 75 years in advance, a provision to which no other government agency or private corporation is subject.  [8] This law, the  Postal Accountability and Enhancement Act, was passed during a  lame-duck session, and shepherded through the  Senate by Senator  Susan Collins of  Maine.  [9] According to Tom Davis (the bill's sponsor), the Bush administration threatened to  veto the legislation unless they added the provision regarding funding the employee benefits in advance with the objective of using that money to reduce the federal deficit.  [10] The agency's struggles were compounded by the  financial crisis of 2007–2008 and  subsequent recession, which caused overall mail volume to fall by one-third, and again by the  COVID-19 pandemic and  its economic effects.  [8]
  Businessman and Trump campaign megadonor  [11]  Louis DeJoy was sworn in as  postmaster general on June 15, 2020; he is the first person since 1992 to assume the position without any prior experience in the  United States Postal Service.  [12]
 Upon taking office DeJoy immediately began taking measures to  reduce costs, such as banning the use of  overtime and extra trips to deliver mail.  [2]  [13]  [14] After criticism, DeJoy admitted that these measures were causing delays in mail delivery, but said they would eventually improve service.  [15] DeJoy also reorganized USPS leadership and staffing.  [16] The changes were described in a memo DeJoy released on August 7, 2020.  [17]
 Hundreds of high-speed  mail sorting machines were dismantled and removed from postal facilities,  [18] raising concerns that mailed ballots for the November 3 election might not reach election offices on time.  [19] It was later explained that such machines are taken offline every year in response to diminishing volumes of letter mail, and that 671 of the machines (about 10 percent of the total) in 49 states had been slated for removal this year, 618 of them by August 1.  [20] After DeJoy's announcement that he would suspend his changes, the Postal Service's director of maintenance instructed employees that "they are not to reconnect/reinstall machines that have previously been disconnected without approval from HQ Maintenance, no matter what direction they are getting from their plant manager."  [20]
 Mail collection boxes were removed from the streets in many cities. However, the Postal Service has been removing mail collection boxes as a cost-cutting measure for years — between 1985 and 2011, the number of mail collection boxes was reduced by 60% — so it remains unclear if the removals are connected to DeJoy's changes.  [21] After photos of boxes being removed were spread on social media, a Postal Service spokesman said they were being moved to higher-traffic areas but that the removals would stop until after the election.  [22]
 On August 18, after weeks of heavy criticism and the day after lawsuits against the Postal Service and DeJoy personally were filed in federal court by several individuals,  [23] DeJoy announced that he would suspend all the changes until after the November election, "to avoid even the appearance of any impact on election mail."  [24] He said he would reinstate overtime hours, roll back service reductions, and halt the removal of mail sorting machines and collection boxes.  [25] However, 95 percent of the mail sorting machines that were planned for removal had already been removed,  [26] and in testimony to the Senate, DeJoy expressed that he had no intention of replacing them.  [27]
  Federal law prohibits any employee of the Postal Service from delaying mail.  [28] The  Postal Service inspector general has opened an investigation into the changes made by DeJoy, including any possible  conflicts of interest stemming from his $30 million ownership stake in  XPO Logistics, which processes mail for the USPS.  [29]  [30]
 Several  state attorneys general are also beginning investigations to determine if DeJoy violated state  election laws  [31] and are coordinating their efforts.  [32] As of August 18, 2020, attorneys general from at least twenty states have filed federal lawsuits against the USPS and DeJoy. One lawsuit is being led by  Washington state, joined by  Colorado,  Connecticut,  Illinois,  Maryland,  Massachusetts,  Michigan,  Minnesota,  New Mexico,  Oregon,  Rhode Island,  Vermont,  Virginia, and  Wisconsin; a second is being led by  Pennsylvania, joined by  California,  Delaware,  Maine, Massachusetts, and  North Carolina. All of the participating attorneys general are  Democrats.  [33] The state lawsuits were filed in spite of DeJoy's announcement that he would be suspending the changes.  [34] On September 17, 2020, federal judge  Stanley A. Bastian, in response to the lawsuit led by Washington state, issued a temporary injunction barring the Postal Service from proceeding with the planned changes, describing them as "a politically motivated attack on the efficiency of the postal service."  [35]
 U.S. representative  Bill Pascrell requested the  New Jersey attorney general to empanel a  grand jury to investigate whether any state election laws were violated; the AG declined comment.  [32]  Arizona secretary of state  Katie Hobbs said, "In Arizona, it's against the law to 'delay the delivery of a ballot.' I've asked  Attorney General Brnovich to investigate recent changes at USPS, and whether or not the  Trump administration has committed a crime."  [32]
 A number of individuals, including candidates for public office, have filed lawsuits in  federal court against the Postal Service, DeJoy and Trump in response to the crisis, and many more are expected.  [23]
 Questions have been raised by observers and journalists about a possible conflict of interest involving DeJoy's connections to  XPO Logistics. The company received $14 million in contracts in ten weeks between June and September 2020. The figure for the corresponding period in 2019 was $3.4 million.  [36]
 On November 4, 2020, federal judge  Emmet G. Sullivan ordered DeJoy to ″sweep″ USPS facilities for undelivered mail-in ballots and to immediately deliver any they find. When DeJoy failed to comply, judge Sullivan announced that DeJoy would have to be ″deposed″.  [37]  [38]  [39]  [40]
   Voting by mail has become an increasingly common practice in the United States. The share of voters nationwide who submit their ballots by mail has grown from about 8 percent in  1996 to about 21 percent in  2016.  [41] Members of the military, their families, and military contractors are able to vote by mail if they are not currently stationed in their permanent home location; more than 600,000 such ballots were cast in the 2016 election.  [42] The  coronavirus pandemic of 2020 is predicted to cause a large increase in mail voting because of the possible danger of congregating at polling places.  [43]  [44] For the 2020 election, a state-by-state analysis concluded that 76 percent of Americans are eligible to vote by mail in 2020, a record number. The analysis predicted that 80 million ballots could be cast by mail in 2020, more than double the number in 2016.  [45] Because of the operational changes causing delays in mail delivery, the Postal Service sent a letter to 46  U.S. states in July 2020, warning each state that the service might not be able to meet that state's deadlines for requesting and casting absentee ballots.  [46]
  Starting in April 2020,  President  Donald Trump began to speak out against voting by mail, claiming without evidence  [47] that it is subject to massive  fraud, and threatening to take steps to prevent states from sending absentee ballots to voters.  [48] Despite his unverified  [47] claims of fraud, Trump himself voted by mail in recent elections, as did  First Lady  Melania Trump and many senior administration officials.  [49] On August 13, 2020, in an interview with  Fox Business Network's  Maria Bartiromo, Trump said he was blocking emergency funding for the USPS because of his opposition to mail-in voting.  [50] Trump later defended the changes being made by DeJoy, saying that DeJoy wanted to "make the Post Office great again", a reference to Trump's own political slogan, " Make America Great Again".  [51] Former U.S. president  Barack Obama accused Trump of deliberately " kneecapping" the Postal Service in order to  discourage people from voting.  [52]
 Contrary to Trump's stated belief that vote by mail harms  Republicans,  [53] there is no evidence that it benefits one party or the other, and both parties have incorporated it into their  get-out-the-vote strategies for years.  [54]  [55] However, nonpartisan experts have warned that delays in the delivery of mail-in ballots could be used by Trump to "steal the election" in the event that more Democrats than Republicans choose to vote by mail in the  November 2020 elections.  [56] Public polling indicates that more Democrats plan to  vote by mail in the elections than Republicans, possibly due to Trump's false claims of vote by mail being riddled with fraud.  [56]  [57] In addition, there is a recurring phenomenon in U.S. elections called the " blue shift", whereby in-person votes tallied on election night favor Republicans, then later-tallied ballots (including mailed ballots) shift the final result toward the Democrats.  [58] Longtime Republican elections lawyer and former  Federal Election Commission chairman  Trevor Potter stated that under such a scenario, Trump could build up a lead among ballots cast on Election Day, claim victory, and then say, "stop counting ballots because all those absentee ballots are illegitimate."  [56] DeJoy denied accusations from members of Congress and  voting rights advocates that the delivery delays were designed to sabotage election mail on Trump's behalf.  [59]
 Trump met with DeJoy at the  White House on August 3, but when initially asked on August 9, Trump denied that he had spoken to DeJoy.  [60] After reports emerged on August 14 of the August 3 meeting, White House spokesman  Judd Deere described it as "congratulatory",  [60] and Trump on August 17 denied having instructed DeJoy to slow mail delivery, saying he "wouldn't do that."  [61]
  Some companies have complained that the delays are hurting their business, since their reputation is dependent on being able to deliver their wares reliably and promptly. A Wisconsin businessman said that with the recent changes, some shipments that might once have been no more than a day late now go missing for weeks, and Priority Mail and Express Mail can now take a week or more.  [62] The problems are especially severe for small businesses.  [63]
 Individuals are also reporting serious economic problems due to delayed or missing mail: not receiving checks or important papers that they are expecting, or having their bill payments arrive at the company late so that they get charged a penalty. In some cities people report getting no mail at all for two or three weeks, and standing in line at the post office to see if it is there.  [64]
  Most prescribed medications from the  Veterans Administration are sent by mail. Those medications are supposed to arrive within three to five days of being mailed.  [65] Since the operational changes imposed by DeJoy, veterans complain that such prescription deliveries have been delayed, often by weeks. Other health essentials such as oxygen tubes are also reportedly being delayed for weeks.  [66] A letter to DeJoy from 31 U.S. senators asked him to reassess the effect of his changes on veterans.  [67]
  Maine farmers reported that thousands of baby chicks had died in the mail after delays at the processing center in  Shrewsbury,  Massachusetts.  [68]  [69] After overtime was cut at a  South Los Angeles sorting facility, packages piled up and blocked machines, rodents were swarming around containers of rotted fruit and meat, baby chicks were found dead inside their boxes, and the building filled with gnats.  [70]
  DeJoy ordered changes with little input from Postal Service workers or their labor unions.  [71] In multiple states, postal workers and union leaders reported that the changes resulted in low worker morale.  [71]
 Some postal workers, aiming to prevent adverse effects on the election and on customer service, resisted the changes ordered by DeJoy.  [71] For example, in New York, some mechanics disregarded an order to dismantle and remove a mail-sorting machine; in Michigan, some letter carriers disregarded an order to leave election mail behind; and in Ohio, some postal workers took steps to ensure that delayed prescriptions and benefit payments were delivered.  [71] Workers in at least two plants in  Washington state reassembled their letter-sorting machines in the week that DeJoy announced he would suspend his changes, despite the Postal Service's national order not to reinstall the machines.  [72]
 Mark Dimondstein, president of the  American Postal Workers Union, criticized the DeJoy orders. Asked in August 2020 whether he believed DeJoy was attempting "to sabotage the election by making cuts that slow down the flow of mail," Dimondstein said: "I can't really judge the motivation. We just have to look at the deeds and the deeds thus far is the new Postmaster General has instituted a number of policies that are truly slowing down mail. The customers see it. We're hearing from customers all over the country. The postal workers see it. Mail is getting backed up."  [73]
  The crisis precipitated  protests against the changes made by DeJoy, with protesters calling for the postmaster general's  resignation. One protest took place on August 15 outside of DeJoy's home in  Washington, D.C.; a second took place on August 16 at his home in  Greensboro, North Carolina.  [74]  [75]  [76]
  On August 16, 2020,  Speaker of the House  Nancy Pelosi ended a recess of the  House of Representatives that had been scheduled to last until the week of September 14, calling lawmakers back to Washington to address the crisis.  [77]  [78] She proposed an early vote on a bill to roll back the changes introduced by DeJoy, which Democrats have described as "a grave threat to the integrity of the election."  [79] Pelosi said that "Lives, livelihoods and the life of our American democracy are under threat from the president."  [80]
 Senate Minority Leader  Chuck Schumer demanded that Majority Leader  Mitch McConnell bring the  Senate back into session also, to consider ways to "undo the extensive damage Mr. DeJoy has done at the Postal Service," but there was no indication McConnell would do so.  [79] Some Republican senators have also called for reversing the changes, pointing out the importance of the Postal Service to  rural residents; they include both senators from  Montana and Senator  Susan Collins of  Maine.  [81]  [82]
 On August 17, 2020, DeJoy agreed to testify before the  House Committee on Oversight and Reform on August 24, and the next day he agreed to testify before the  Senate Committee on Homeland Security and Government Affairs on August 21.  [61]  USPS Board of Governors chairman  Mike Duncan also agreed to testify at the House hearing.  [83]
 Despite DeJoy's announcement that he would be suspending the operational changes until after the election, Pelosi stated that she did not trust DeJoy, and called the suspension "a necessary but insufficient step in ending the president’s election sabotage campaign."  [24]
 On August 21, 2020, DeJoy testified before the U.S. Senate that he votes by mail.  [84] He committed to senators at the Senate Homeland Security and Governmental Affairs Committee that voters should be "extremely highly confident" that even mail-in ballots sent close to Election Day would be delivered on time,  [85] and promised that the USPS would respect its "sacred duty" to deliver election mail in the fall of 2020.  [86] Senator  Jacky Rosen asked DeJoy to provide the sources of data collected, data analyses, and considerations of consequences—specifically on veterans dependent on the mail for their prescriptions—that DeJoy had undertaken before implementing the removal of mail sorting machines, "reduction, elimination of overtime and late trips".  [87]  [85] He said he was unable to do so.  [87]
 On August 22, 2020, the House passed a bill approving $25 billion in emergency funding for the USPS that would reverse DeJoy's changes and ban mail delays before the election. The vote was 257-150 as Democrats approved it unanimously and 26 Republicans joined them. The White House threatened a  veto.  [88]  [89]  [90]
 Congresswoman  Debbie Wasserman Schultz, a member of the  House Committee on Oversight and Reform, was denied access to two post offices in Florida when she tried to conduct an inspection on September 3.  [91]
  In December 2020, the holiday season package rush resulted in widespread package shipping delays nationwide. USPS facilities became overwhelmed with packages: A supervisor at an Indianapolis distribution center reported that for the sake of expediency, workers were "just throwing [packages] in the trucks without scanning them" into the system;  [92] in Allentown, Pennsylvania, mailpieces sat in the parking lot because there was no room to unload them, and a clerk in Philadelphia told the  Philadelphia Inquirer "Don’t be using the post office right now, because we can’t deliver the mail".  [93] Late in December, USPS acknowledged the delays on its website citing COVID-19 issues and a labor shortage and encouraging early mailing of packages, but for many with packages already trapped at distribution centers across the country it was too little, too late.  [94]  [95]  [96]  [97]

title: 2020年美国邮政服务危机

note:
加利福尼亚州不关心，因为世界上第四大富豪搬到奥斯汀，这似乎很奇怪。但是，我们持乐观态度有两个原因：我们认识到生活在非常富有的人群中的危害，并且我们比您更了解这位亿万富翁。因此，我们感谢您为避开SpaceX和Tesla的负责人而提出以下建议：
  马斯克的退出与其他加州到德克萨斯的举动不同，我们对此感觉不太好。公司总部的离开-最近是甲骨文，惠普企业（Hewlett Packard Enterprise）和麦凯森（McKesson）-给我们带来了高薪工作，并反映了我们在成本，法规和治理方面的实际问题。更糟糕的是，年轻的工人阶级加利福尼亚人经常离开去达拉斯和休斯顿郊区的廉价住房和更好的学校。
   但是马斯克的退出是完全不同的。尽管许多人因为负担不起而离开加利福尼亚，但马斯克之所以离开是因为加利福尼亚人终于意识到我们负担不起。
 马斯克的身家可能超过1000亿美元，但他的伪善和忘恩负义更加丰富。当他实际上是自铁路大亨以来加州最大的福利案例时，他树立了孤独，自制的创新者的形象。马斯克的三个签名公司-SpaceX，Tesla和SolarCity-建立在数十亿美元的政府合同，补贴和其他巨额资金之上。联邦政府提供了大部分资金，但加利福尼亚州向他提供了大量资金，并制定了有利于其公司出售的电动汽车和太阳能电池板的法规。
 然而，加州的支持并没有使马斯克成为我们州的好公民。马斯克损害了特斯拉在弗里蒙特工厂的工人安全，违反了证券法，破坏了其员工的工会组织。马斯克已成为加利福尼亚的科学怪人；我们的怪物反对我们。
 大流行爆发后，他对加利福尼亚的举动变得残酷无情。他抨击了数百万加利福尼亚人所需的联邦经济救济计划，同时也从这些计划中受益。他指责加州的COVID反应是法西斯主义者，而他仍然与怀恨加利福尼亚的白宫独裁者保持友好关系。
  最糟糕的是，马斯克无视家里的订单，重新开了特斯拉在弗里蒙特的工厂，这可能在那儿造成了COVID-19爆发。马斯克不屈不挠，扬言要离开德克萨斯。然后他进行了威胁。 
在出门时，他打破了chutzpah的所有世界纪录。他声称加利福尼亚州不支持创新-尽管我们给予了他所有的支持。他将自己的离开描述为一场正义的抗议活动-从未提及得克萨斯州缺乏所得税，以及他最近的补偿金增加。他甚至还给罐子打了个电话，指控加利福尼亚“有资格”。
 但是他的无耻品牌推卸责任似乎对您来说很完美，德克萨斯。要求最高法院取消数百万其他州的选票的州，同时又要求批准总统的选择，对一个亿万富翁来说是一个合适的住所，他拒绝其他人为他致富的政府援助。
 他出卖您时不要感到惊讶。尽管他经常无法兑现承诺的职位数量，但您的社区仍在资助他的业务。他一定会抨击您的政客和石油工业。一些评论家注意到，德克萨斯州刚刚欢迎美国公司福利之王。
 目前，SpaceX和Tesla总部将留在加利福尼亚。但是，如果马斯克最终将它们带走，那可能只是短期打击。如果政府的支持枯竭，或者马斯克在法律上遇到更深的麻烦，那么这些公司将成为纸牌屋。而且，得克萨斯州必须处理任何麝香崩溃的大屠杀。
 孤星领袖通常会警告不要对加利福尼亚万物的涌入。但是，得克萨斯州，您似乎并不担心马斯克及其公司。
 同时，加州只有一个新的担忧理由：与最近进行测试的SpaceX的Falcon 9一样，马斯克将试图回到我们身边……最终陷入火海。

It may seem strange for California to not care as the world’s fourth-richest person relocates to Austin. But we’re sanguine for two reasons: We recognize the hazards of living amongst the very rich, and we know this billionaire better than you. So with our thanks for giving refuge to the SpaceX and Tesla chief comes this advice:
  Musk’s exit is different than other California-to-Texas moves, about which we feel less good. The departures of company headquarters — recently Oracle, Hewlett Packard Enterprise,  McKesson — cost us high-wage jobs, and reflect real problems with our costs, regulations and governance. Even worse, younger working-class Californians often leave for the cheaper housing and better schools of suburban Dallas and Houston.
   But Musk’s exit is entirely different. While many people leave California because they can’t afford it, Musk is leaving because Californians finally figured out we couldn’t afford him.
 Musk may be worth over $100 billion — but he’s even richer in hypocrisy and ingratitude. He cultivated the image of the lone, self-made innovator, when he was actually California’s biggest welfare case since the  railroad barons. Musk’s three signature companies — SpaceX, Tesla, and SolarCity — are built on billions of dollars in government contracts, subsidies and other largesse. The federal government provided much of this, but California has showered him with money, and provided regulations favoring the electric cars and solar panels his companies sell.
 Yet California’s support did not make Musk a good citizen of our state. Musk  compromised worker safety at Tesla’s Fremont plant,  flouted securities laws and sabotaged unionization of his employees. Musk has become California’s Frankenstein; our monster turned against us.
 And when the pandemic hit, his behavior toward California turned unforgivably cruel. He railed against the federal economic relief packages that millions of Californians needed — while benefiting from those same packages. He accused California’s COVID response of being fascist while he remained friendly with the California-hating authoritarian in the White House.
  Worst of all, Musk defied stay-at-home orders to reopen Tesla’s factory in Fremont, which may have produced a  COVID-19 outbreak there. Unbowed, Musk threatened to leave for Texas. Then he carried out the threat.
 On his way out, he broke all world records for chutzpah. He claimed California doesn’t support innovation — despite all the backing we gave him.  He portrayed his departure as a righteous protest — never mentioning Texas’ lack of income taxes, and recent increases in his compensation package. He even had the pot-calls-you-the-kettle gall to accuse California of being “entitled.”
 But his brash brand of blame-shifting seems perfect for you, Texas. The state that asked the Supreme Court to cancel millions of other states’ votes — while demanding its presidential choice be ratified — is a fitting home for a billionaire who would deny others the government assistance that made him rich.
 Just don’t be surprised when he betrays you. Your communities are subsidizing his businesses, even though he often falls short of promised jobs numbers. He’s sure to lash out at your politicians and oil industry.  A few editorialists have noticed that Texas just welcomed America’s corporate welfare king.
 SpaceX and Tesla headquarters will remain in California — for now. But if Musk eventually take them with him, it might only be a short-term blow. If government support dries up, or if Musk gets in deeper trouble with the law, those companies could become houses of cards. And, Texas would have to handle the carnage of any Musk meltdown.
 Lone Star leaders typically warn against the influx of all things California. But, Texas, you don’t seem worried — yet — about Musk and his companies.
 California, meanwhile, has only one new reason to worry: Much like SpaceX’s Falcon 9 in recent testing, Musk will attempt to return to us … and end up in flames.
title: 马修斯：得克萨斯州，谢谢您摆脱Elon Musk的控制
title_en: Mathews: Thank you, Texas, for taking Elon Musk off our hands

核倡导者认为，碳捕获和碳基产品中的反应堆有广阔的市场，不仅是正在开发的下一代反应堆，还包括从中演化出来的老化恐龙。
  “碳产品代表了一个全新的核能市场的潜力，”地球能源公司首席财务官佳能·布莱恩说，该公司正在开发一种使用液态氟化铀燃料的反应堆。
  “传统上，核能只限于发电领域，这很好，甚至可能会进行淡化，但是这些类型的工业产品和工业发电代表了下一代核技术的新市场。”
    在这些碳产品中，有可持续的航空燃料，目前主要由生物质制成。核工业会从烟囱（可能是在水泥厂）获取这种燃料的碳，如果成本降幅足够大，则最终会从直接空气捕获中获取碳。
    一些拥护者还认为，现有反应堆正朝着淘汰的方向发展。例如，加利福尼亚州圣路易斯奥比斯波的暗黑破坏神峡谷核电站计划于2024年和2025年关闭。
    “如果将这家工厂的废热与电力生产结合起来，那么每年可以从大气中清除2000万吨的碳，”人类能源能源联合创始人Kirsty Gogan在EarthX小组上说。
  “目前正在发生的事是这些巨大的千兆瓦级折旧资产，它们正在提供基本负荷，清洁的，无排放的电力，但是我们只是在浪费热量，对吧？这些核电站可能会更有用，为我们所有人必须负起的责任做出了巨大贡献。 
“我们都试图保持中立，但这还不够好。我们必须对已经存在于大气中的碳负起责任，并且要消极。”
  核倡导者认为，先进反应堆产生的热量不仅是捕集碳的方法，而且是逃避了一些使美国核工业陷入瘫痪的高资本成本的方法。
  布莱恩说，设计仅产生热量用于碳捕集的反应堆不会产生将热量转化为电能的设备的资本成本，“既为下一代核能创造了新的生命，同时也为公众提供了服务，并为每个参与其中的人谋利。因此，我认为这可能是一个善意的循环。”
  Gogan说，模块化反应堆也许也更便宜，因为它们可以在工厂以标准型号制造：
  “如果您从低生产率的构建方法转变为高生产率的制造环境，那么标准化的产品专为制造和组装而设计，可重复使用，因此您不必每次都在重新设计产品，就可以实现非常高生产率的制造和交付—您最终会得到非常短的施工进度，对预算和进度有很多确定性，并最终获得了优化的结果，就像您期望在任何标准化制造产品中看到的那样。”
note_en:
Nuclear advocates see a vast market for reactors in carbon capture and carbon-based products, not only for the next generation of reactors in development, but also for the aging dinosaurs they evolved from.
  “Carbon products represent the potential for an entirely new market for nuclear energy,” said Canon Bryan, the CFO of Terrestrial Energy, which is developing a reactor that uses liquid uranium fluoride fuel.
  “Nuclear energy has traditionally been constrained to the province of electric-power generation, and that’s been fine, and maybe a little bit of desalination, but these types of industrial products and industrial generation represent new markets for the next generation of nuclear technologies.”
    Among those carbon products is sustainable aviation fuel, currently made mostly from biomass. The nuclear industry would derive the carbon for that fuel instead from smokestacks, likely at cement plants, and eventually, if costs drop sufficiently, from direct-air capture.
    Some advocates also see a new market for the existing reactors that are limping toward obsolescence. The Diablo Canyon Nuclear Power Plant in San Luis Obispo, California, for example, is slated to shut down in 2024 and 2025.
    “If the waste heat from that plant was being combined with electricity production you could be removing 20 million tons per year of carbon from the atmosphere,” said Kirsty Gogan, co-founder of Energy for Humanity, at an  EarthX panel on Wednesday.
  “Right now what’s happening is these big gigawatt-scale depreciating assets—they’re making baseload, clean, emissions-free power, but we’re just throwing away the heat, right? Those nuclear plants could be more useful, making a big contribution toward that responsibility we all have to go negative.
  “We all try to be neutral, but it ain’t good enough. We have to take responsibility for the carbon that’s already in the atmosphere and go negative.”
  Nuclear advocates see the heat from advanced reactors not only as a  way to capture carbon, but as a way to escape some of the high capital costs that have crippled the nuclear industry in the U.S.
  Reactors designed to produce just heat for carbon capture won’t incur the capital cost of the equipment that converts heat into electricity, Bryan said, “both creating a new life for next-generation nuclear and also doing a public service at the same time, and making a profit for everybody involved. So I think it’s a benevolent cycle, potentially.”
  Modular reactors may also be cheaper, Gogan said, because they can be manufactured in factories on a standard model:
  “If you move away from the sort of low-productivity construction approach to a high-productivity manufacturing environment—standardized products, designed for manufacture and assembly, designed for reuse so you don’t redesign your product every time, very high productivity manufacturing and delivery—you end up with really short construction schedules, a lot of certainty around budget and schedule and ultimately optimized outcomes, as you’d expect to see with any standardized manufactured product.”

 富人和穷人的饮食差异很大。上层阶级及其家庭享受新鲜和进口食品，而其余人口则不得不依靠当地的土地为生，在冬季末或短缺的时候，土地可能很少！
  饮食不仅受季节的影响，宗教也影响了人们的饮食。星期五（在早期，星期三和星期六）是每周必须禁食或“ fysshe”的日子，禁止吃肉。也有每年的禁食，例如节食日，降临节和四旬斋，它们限制饮食。中世纪的厨师在很短的时间内为富有的食客们发明了创意食谱–包括禁止由乳制品制成的由彩色杏仁糊制成的模拟煮熟的鸡蛋，放在大炮弹中，用于四旬期。
  宴会的准备工作涉及大量准备工作。当整个皇室集会时，数百人可能会坐下来吃饭。对于复活节和圣诞节的两次盛宴，当订购和制作蜜饯时，准备工作必须在几个月前开始。禁食发生在复临时期，这意味着要准备四个星期的瘦肉来准备盛宴。
